cycles.name: Bedrock
cycles.tagline: Build the base, earn the load.
focuses: Calibrate every load and re-pattern the hinge trunk-supported — spine-safe glute foundation.
Zone-2 aerobic base on the run day: cap the heart rate, slow the pace, grow the engine.

paragraphs: This cycle lays the two foundations everything else gets built on: glutes that respond to load, and an engine that doesn't redline at an easy pace. The hip thrust is your anchor lift — we're going to calibrate it, groove it, and start loading it week by week, with every other exercise chosen to support that goal while keeping your spine completely out of the argument.

Mental focus for these 5 weeks: precision over heroics. Week 1 will feel easy on purpose — you're finding your own working weights against the RPE targets, not me assigning them. Log every set honestly and the program sharpens itself around what you report.
By the end of Bedrock: heavier hip thrusts than you've ever done, a hinge pattern ready to earn the next cycle's new lift, and a run pace that finally matches your heart instead of fighting it.
outcomes: Hip thrust loaded and progressing week over week
Working weights dialed in through your own RPE feedback
Zone-2 run built from 35 to ~50 minutes
Lower average HR at the same easy pace
Hinge pattern grooved — next cycle's key lift earned
5-day rhythm locked in around real life

cycles.name: Volume Engine
focuses: Glute hypertrophy volume build on the calibrated patterns; the loaded free hinge arrives.
Extend zone-2 duration; HR at a fixed pace becomes the tracked fitness marker.

paragraphs: Bedrock builds the platform. What comes next is the payoff phase every glute goal actually requires — more volume, aimed exactly where you want it, on patterns you'll have already earned. There's one lift waiting in Cycle 2 that only unlocks if these five weeks are done right.
First you build the base. Then we turn up the volume.

cycles.name: Strength Engine
focuses: Progressive overload — push intensity on the glute drivers built in Cycle 2.
Steady-state runs lengthen; expect a visibly lower HR at Cycle 1's pace.

cycles.name: Metabolic Override
focuses: Density + conditioning emphasis for the fat-loss goal while holding glute strength.
Run day adds controlled tempo touches on top of the base.

cycles.name: Capstone
focuses: Peak glute volume + intensity on the best-responding movers from the whole arc.
Consolidate aerobic gains; end-of-arc reassessment against the starting run baseline.

days.completionMessage: That thrust number is the one we build the whole program around — logged and climbing. Protein within a couple of hours, and tomorrow the upper body gets its turn.

good: Walk tall and push the belt away
Warm, not winded
bad: Don't grip the rails

good: Push the floor away as you round
Move one vertebra at a time
bad: Don't force the low back into a deep arch

good: Drive heels through the floor
Squeeze the glutes at the top, not the low back
bad: Don't finish by arching the spine

good: Push the ground sideways
Feel the side of the hip burn
bad: Don't let the knees cave in

good: Drive the bar to the ceiling with your hips
Glutes finish the rep — squeeze them like you're cracking a walnut
bad: Don't hyperextend the low back at the top — ribs stay down, chin tucked

good: Push the sled away through mid-foot and heel
Feel quads and glutes share the work
bad: Never let the low back peel off the pad at the bottom — cut depth before that happens

good: Sweep the leg back and slightly up
One glute does everything — the standing leg just balances
bad: Don't swing from the low back to move more weight

good: Push the ankle straight out to the side against the cable
Feel the side of the hip, not the low back
bad: Don't lean the torso away to cheat the range

good: Press straight out and freeze — beat the cable's pull
Brace like someone's about to poke your stomach
bad: Don't let the hips or shoulders twist toward the stack

days.completionMessage: Presses and rows done with your spine fully supported — strength built, back protected. Tomorrow is the slow run: leave the ego at home and keep it under 145.

good: Smooth circles
Raise the temperature
bad: Don't race it

good: Wide grip, big slow arc
Open the chest
bad: Don't bend the elbows to cheat the arc

good: Stretch the band to your chest
Squeeze between the shoulder blades
bad: Don't shrug into your ears

good: Push the floor away as you round
Segment by segment
bad: Don't rush the extension

good: Press both bells to the ceiling until elbows lock
Shoulders do the work — not the neck
bad: Don't arch off the backrest to grind a rep — the backrest protects your spine, use it

good: Pull the bells to your hips, elbows brushing your sides
Shoulder blades start every rep
bad: Don't lift the chest off the pad for momentum

good: Press up and slightly back over the collarbones
Upper chest stretches at the bottom, squeezes at the top
bad: Don't flare the elbows straight out — keep them about 45 degrees

good: Row the handle to your hip pocket
The lat drives, the arm just connects
bad: Don't rotate the torso open to yank the weight

good: Curl straight up without swinging
Biceps do the lifting, elbows stay pinned to your sides
bad: Don't rock the torso to sling the weight up

good: Push the bar down until arms are straight
Triceps lock out the last inch
bad: Don't let the elbows drift forward away from your ribs

good: Walk a perfectly straight line, tall
The free side of your waist fights the lean
bad: Don't let the weight tip your shoulder down

days.completionMessage: Every minute under that HR ceiling was a deposit into your aerobic base — the slowness IS the training. Watch that pace-at-145 number over the next five weeks.

good: Arms swinging, building pace
Legs waking up
bad: Don't jump straight into the run cold

good: Swing tall holding a support — front-to-back, then side-to-side
Hips loosening with each swing
bad: Don't round the back to swing higher

good: Rock the knee over the toes, heel glued down
Feel the ankle open up, not the calf stretch
bad: Don't let the arch collapse inward

good: Drive heels through the floor
Wake the glutes up before they do the work of holding you upright
bad: Don't finish by arching the spine

good: Step back onto a rail, tall through the chest
Front leg controls the whole movement
bad: Don't let the front knee dive inward

good: Light and quick — pop off the ground
Feel the rhythm build, not the effort
bad: Don't turn it into a sprint — this primes the pattern, it isn't the workout

good: Run slow enough to hold a full conversation out loud
This should feel almost embarrassingly easy — that's the target, not a failure
bad: Don't chase pace — if HR passes 145, walk until it drops under 135, then resume

days.completionMessage: Split squats and single-leg bridges — each glute earned its own paycheck today, and that pull-through is quietly unlocking next cycle's lift. Eat, stretch, sleep.

good: Smooth circles
Warm legs
bad: Don't sprint it

good: Rotate both knees side to side
Hips opening, spine quiet
bad: Don't force range with the low back

good: Heels through the floor
Glutes, not low back
bad: Don't arch to finish

good: Step back onto a rail
Front leg owns the movement
bad: Don't let the front knee dive inward

good: Drop the back knee straight down to the floor
The front glute stretches at the bottom and drives you up
bad: Don't bounce out of the bottom — control down, drive up

good: Drive through the whole foot on the box, not a push off the back leg
The top glute finishes the rep, standing tall
bad: Don't let the back leg do the work — that's cheating the stimulus you came here for

good: Push your hips back to the wall behind you, then snap them through
Hamstrings load like rubber bands, spine stays a straight bar
bad: Never round the low back — the hips move, the spine doesn't

good: Curl the pad toward your heels
Hamstrings do all the work — hips stay pinned to the seat
bad: Don't let the hips rise off the pad to cheat the rep

good: Drive one heel through the floor to lift the hips level
One glute lifts your whole body
bad: Don't let the pelvis tilt or drop to one side

good: Make a straight ramp from shoulders to feet
The bottom side of your waist holds everything
bad: Don't let the hips sag toward the floor

days.completionMessage: Lightest day of the week, and it still moved you forward — pattern practice, pump, and a core that protects your spine. Five for five. That consistency is the real program.

good: Smooth and light
Loosen the week off
bad: Don't turn it into cardio

good: Long lunge, reach to the ceiling
Hips and mid-back opening
bad: Don't collapse into the front hip

good: Heels down, hips up
Glutes on
bad: No low-back arch

good: Band to chest
Shoulder blades squeeze
bad: Don't shrug

good: Sit straight down between your heels
Quads stretch, torso stays tall the whole way
bad: Keep it LIGHT — this squat is practice, not a test; stop the set if the low back speaks up

good: Drive the bell up with your hips
Glutes squeeze hard at the top
bad: Don't finish with the low back

good: Push the floor away until elbows lock
Whole body moves as one plank — stop 2 reps short of failure
bad: Don't let the hips sag — that's your spine flexing under load

good: Pull the rope to your eyebrows, elbows high and wide
Rear shoulders and shoulder blades finish the pull
bad: Don't turn it into a row — elbows stay above shoulder line

good: Press through the balls of your feet as high as possible
Feel the calf fully squeeze at the top
bad: Don't bounce out of the bottom stretch

good: Reach one arm and the opposite heel to opposite walls
Nothing moves except the reaching limbs — hips stay level
bad: Don't let the low back arch as the leg extends

good: Forearms down, make one straight line
Glutes and abs both squeezing
bad: Don't sag through the hips or pike them up

cards.title: Your Heart Rate Isn't "Natural"
cards.body:
On your July 4th run you averaged 177 bpm at an easy-conversation pace — that's not "just how your body is," it's a sign your aerobic base is underbuilt, and it's very fixable.

That's why your run day has a hard ceiling of 145 bpm: run slow enough to talk out loud, and the moment your watch crosses 145, walk until it drops under 135, then resume. Walk breaks are the program working, not you failing.

Do this for five weeks and watch what happens to your pace at the same heart rate — that number dropping is your engine growing.

cards.title: The Glute Rule: Feel It There
cards.body:
Your goal is written all over this program — glutes get touched on three of your four lifting days, with the hip thrust as the anchor.

One rule on every glute exercise: if you feel it in your low back, the set is over — reset your ribs down, squeeze the glutes to finish the rep, and go again lighter.

A set of 8 you feel entirely in your glutes beats a set of 12 your back helped with. That's how the load climbs safely week after week.

cards.title: Your Spine Rules, Built In
cards.body:
Everything here already respects your three rules:

  • No heavy squatting — the leg press and goblet squat stay light-to-moderate; they're pattern work, not tests.
  • Nothing lying on your back raising your legs — your core work is planks, bird dogs, Pallof presses and carries instead, genuinely better for your back anyway.
  • Pain means stop, message me — your own rule, and it stands.

You've been pain-free and I intend to keep you that way. Strong glutes and a braced core are the best insurance your back can own.

cards.title: Pain Rules For Your Back History
cards.body:
Your L4-L5 history means "listen to your back" needs to be more specific than that — here's exactly what to do.

What's OK: normal muscle fatigue, the burn of a hard set, general next-day stiffness. That's training working, not your back complaining — keep going.

What's not OK: anything sharp, anything that shoots down a leg, numbness or tingling anywhere, or pain that builds as the set goes on instead of just being effort.

  • Mild, and it fades right after: finish the set, note it in your log.
  • Building, or changing how you move: stop that exercise, swap to the fallback listed on the card, message me.
  • Sharp, radiating, numb, or unfamiliar: stop the whole session and message me before your next one — don't push through it or wait it out.

Your own rule already stands: pain means stop, message me. This is just the detail behind it.

cards.title: How To Progress & Regress
cards.body:
The app never hands you a weight — you find it every time against the RPE target.

New movement? Pick something conservative and let the first set's RPE tell you whether to go up or come down.

Same exercise, next week? Hit the prescribed reps at or under the target RPE → add a small jump — enough to feel it, not enough to miss reps. RPE right at the ceiling or reps missed → hold that weight and repeat before adding more.

Bad day? Regression isn't failure. On a rough-readiness day, or if a set is grinding harder than the target RPE says it should, drop the weight — that's the system working, not you failing.

cards.title: Long Days: The Readiness Rule
cards.body:
Your life-load is heavy — long days, little downtime — so this program bends instead of breaking.

Do the readiness check before every session, honestly. On a rough day: drop every RPE by one, and if time or energy collapses, do just the first main lift and leave — that counts as a completed session, not a failure.

You can also reorder days within the week. Five imperfect sessions beat three perfect ones and two skipped.

cards.title: Period Week: The Fallback Menu
cards.body:
For the hard days of your cycle, don't negotiate with yourself from zero — the plan is already made.

  • Keep in full: the zone-2 run (or swap it for a walk), all warm-up circuits, glute bridges and lateral band walks — light movement genuinely eases cramping.
  • Cut to 2 lighter sets: hip thrust, leg press, split squats, rows and presses — move the pattern, don't grind it.
  • Skip outright: Pallof press, planks, side planks, bird dogs and suitcase carries — anything that squeezes your abs hard.

The goal of that week is protecting the habit, not chasing progress.

cards.title: Protein Anchors The Whole Goal
cards.body:
You said nutrition is mostly on track — here's the one lever that decides whether this cycle builds glutes while losing fat: protein at every meal, roughly a palm-sized portion four times a day (for you that's in the range of 100–120g daily).

Don't overhaul anything else yet. We'll refine the details on our weekly check-ins once I see how your body responds to the first two weeks of training.

cards.title: Make Sleep Quality Match Hours
cards.body:
You get 7–8 hours but rate the quality 6/10 — and with your schedule, sleep is where your training actually turns into results.

One change this cycle: a hard screen-off 30 minutes before bed, same wake time every day, even weekends.

Recovery is the ceiling on everything in this program; raise it and every number in the app climbs faster.
